channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Next Xbox, Project Helix, Will Reportedly Not Have a Disc Drive
item.content:encoded:
The next Xbox, for now known as Project Helix, will not have a disc drive per reporting from Windows Central. The only version of the console will be discless and not accept physical media.

This news is a double blow for video game preservation and physical game enthusiasts, as just yesterday PlayStation announced they would be ending all physical disc production in 2028. And that news came after Rockstar Games announced there will be no disc version of Grand Theft Auto VI, which is gearing up to be one of the biggest game releases of all time.

The biggest outcry to the news and reporting about Xbox surrounds video game preservation. Digital only versions of games make it difficult to archive games for a variety of reasons, such as games getting consistently patched and updated so their original form is changed piece by piece.

In response to the PlayStation news, which applies also here, the director of the Video Game History Foundation, Frank Cifaldi, made a statement on what this sort of news means for game preservation:

This is unfortunate news for those who still prefer buying games on physical media, and is certainly a hit to consumer rights, the resale market, and game creators whose businesses rely on the physical market. But from the perspective of professional preservationists, this doesn't have as much of an impact as you might expect.

The reality is that the vast majority of video games produced over the last two decades were not made for dedicated home video game consoles, let alone pressed to physical media. And even when they were released on physical media, a day-one digital patch was all but guaranteed, meaning that even though a disc is preserving data in an accessible way, it may not represent the game that people actually played. Museums and archives have been preparing for this future for a while, with the expectation that putting discs on a shelf isn't going to be a long-term solution for preserving new games.

What continues to baffle us is what the industry expects institutions like ours to do about it. If platform owners are deciding to eliminate physical media and older digital storefronts, then we'd also like to see trade groups like the Entertainment Software Association offer meaningful solutions for archives and museums to legally preserve digital-only content and make it accessible for research. Everyone agrees this is a serious problem, but the ESA has repeatedly opposed the efforts of cultural heritage institutions to reform digital copy protection laws to make it easier to do this work. The industry needs to meaningfully come to the table on this issue, because asking museums to download a copy of Grand Theft Auto VI and hope it'll run in 50 years is not a preservation solution.

Beyond game preservation, one of the most enduring aspects of gaming is the used game market. In a future of no physical media, no longer will anyone be able to trade, sell, or buy used games.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/next-xbox-project-helix-will-reportedly-not-have-disc-drive
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
